Hedge Funds
Lectures 7

amoroman
 Course Outline
• Hedge funds structure
• Hedge funds vs. mutual funds
• Hedge fund strategies
• Portable alpha and pure play
• Performance measurement for hedge funds
   • Exposure to omitted risk factors
   • Hedge funds and the use of options
• Fee structure in hedge funds
   • High water marks
   • Funds of funds
• Role of hedge funds in the economy
                                    FINS 3640
 Hedge Funds: Useful links
• HFRI indices: https://www.hedgefundresearch.com/family-indices/hfri
• Credit Suisse HF: https://lab.credit-suisse.com/
• Warren Buffet critique to high-end money managers:

• Warren Buffet Hedge Fund bet:
   • http://fortune.com/2017/12/30/warren-buffett-million-dollar-bet/
• Renaissance technologies website: https://www.renfund.com/

     in-11-billion-last-year.html


                                       FINS 3640                                    3
     Hedge Funds - Structure
        • Hedge funds are private investment vehicles open to a limited number of wealthy or professional investors.
            • Limited number of investors. Investors must be accredited or qualified purchasers (legal terms for
              “rich, sophisticated investors”). Minimum capital requirements are high.
            • See Appendix A for the definition of accredited or qualified purchasers under the exemption from 40-
              Act.

     • HFs usually take the form of a limited partnership or limited liability company or trust depending on the
        domicile
          • In the US, hedge funds are structured as limited partnerships
                • General partner (GP): responsible for day-to-day management
security        • Limited partner (LP): investors liable only for amount they invest

        • HFs are structured so they are exempt from regulation under the Investment Company Act of 1940.
            • They only need to comply with § 3(c)(1) or § 3(c)(7)
                  • See Appendix A for the details of Investment Company Act § 3(c)(1) & § 3(c)(7)

        • In contrast, mutual funds are overseen by the SEC because they are issuing publicly traded securities (i.e.,
          not limited to accredited or qualified purchasers)

        • This means HFs have a lot of flexibility about the way they trade, the instruments they can purchase
          (derivatives), taking on short positions, etc… relative to mutual funds

                                                              FINS 3640                                                  4
 Appendix A: Exemption from 40-Act
• HFs usually take the form of a limited partnership or limited liability
  company or trust depending on the domicile
     • Master/feeder structure to minimize taxes for different parties, particularly
       US taxes of the earnings of foreign investors
     • In the US, hedge funds are structured as limited partnerships
        • General partner (GP): responsible for day-to-day management
        • Limited partner (LP): investors liable only for amount they invest
• HFs are structured so they are exempt from regulation under the
     • Investment Company Act of 1940: Comply with § 3(c)(1) or § 3(c)(7)
     • Securities Act 1933: firms issuing publicly traded securities overseen by the
       SEC

                             FINS 3640                          7
 Hedge Fund Strategies
• Directional/Market timing: range from specialists in certain sectors
  to global macro seeking to capture global market trends. This style is
  directional and is net long or short.

• Non-directional: aims to be market neutral and usually extracts value
  from a diversified set of “arbitrage” opportunities.
   • Exploit temporary misalignments in relative valuation across sectors
   • Buy one type of security and sell another similar security
   • Strives to be market neutral

• Both approaches can deliver low correlations with traditional asset
  classes.

   Hedge Fund Trading Decisions


• Discretionary: Relies on a person’s judgment to determine trades.

• Systematic: More rules-based and relies on quantitative models. The
  extreme example is quant black-box high-frequency trading, eg
  Renaissance.




                                FINS 3640                               9
 Strategy: Convergence and relative valuation
• Convergence versus relative valuation: Involves the purchase of an
  undervalued security and the simultaneous short sale of an
  overvalued security.
   • Both seek to exploit temporary misalignments in prices.
   • Convergence expects the misalignment to occur within a pre-specified time
     frame (ex: futures price versus spot price).


• Example: Convergence Trade in Fixed income – Classic LTCM Trade
   • 30-year bonds, when issued (“on-the-run”), are usually more liquid than 29.5-
     year bonds
   • Yield is lower (price higher)
   • Convergence trade: buy a 29.5 year bond and sell the 30-year bond.

 Convergence Trade in Fixed income
• Relative value – fixed income
    • On-the-run vs off-the-run is an example of a convergence trade. Newly issued
      Treasuries are on-the-run and are most liquid and tend to trade at higher
      prices (lower yields) than off-the-run Treasuries.

14                                                                       FINS 3640                                                                 14
 Market Neutral Long-Short & Pure Play: Portable
Alpha
   1. Invest wherever you can find alpha
   2. Hedge the systematic risk of the investment to
      isolate its alpha
   3. Establish exposure to desired market sectors by
      using passive products such as indexed mutual
      funds, ETFs, or index futures
       •   Transfer alpha from the sector where you find it to the
           asset class in which you ultimately establish exposure

   •   Such strategies are not risk-free arbitrage strategies
       but rather statistical arbitrage (i.e., Pure play).

  Performance Measurement for Hedge Funds
• Possible sources of superior performance
    • Skilled managers

   • Exposure to omitted risk factors with positive risk premiums:
       • Liquidity
       • Tail events
       • Market timing implies straddle type returns.

   • Survivorship bias: HFs with poor performance stop reporting
       • https://www.dolphins-world.com/dolphins-rescuing-humans/
   • Backfill bias: HFs will wait for a good track record before starting to report.

   • Changing factor loadings     Market timing implies changes in B over time

       Liquidity and Hedge Fund Performance
• Hedge funds tend to hold more illiquid assets than other institutional
  investors
                                Ls capture iliquidity premia
• Aragon (2007, JFE):
   • Funds with more lock-up periods tend to hold illiquid securities.
   • Controlling for lock-up periods alphas become insignificant.
   • Typical alpha may actually be an equilibrium liquidity premium rather than a sign of
     stock-picking ability


• Hasanhodzic and Lo: Hedge fund returns have serial correlation, a sign of
  liquidity problems, which explains the upward bias in the Sharpe ratios

                       FINS 3640                                       22
           Liquidity and Hedge Fund Performance
• Sadka (2010, JFE): Unexpected declines in market liquidity are an
  important determinant of average hedge fund returns. Funds with
  higher liquidity-risk betas have higher returns (about 6% annually).

• Santa effect: Hedge funds report average returns in December that
  are substantially greater than their average returns in other months.
  This enhances their annual incentive fees…
   • The December spike in returns is stronger for lower-liquidity funds.
   • Suggests funds buy additional shares of stocks they already own to push up
     their prices. Especially at the close of the last day of the month.

         Hedge Fund Performance and biases
• Backfill bias:         a       is    overestimated too big
                             a
   • Hedge funds report returns only if they choose to, and they may do so only
     when their prior performance is good

                                         performance drop out
• Survivorship bias:      S HF with poor
   • Failed funds drop out of the database
                                                    Fistoobig
   • Hedge fund attrition rates are more than double those for mutual funds

• Selection bias
   • The best HFs do not report to databases, eg Renaissance Medallion, Caxton,
     DE Shaw, SAC, Citadel, etc

                                      FINS 3640                                   25
       Hedge Fund Performance and Changing
edits Factor Loadings B
   • Hedge funds are designed to be opportunistic and may frequently
     change their risk profiles
      • If risk is not constant, alphas will be biased in the standard linear index
        model

   • Conclusions
      • Perfect market timing results in a nonlinear characteristic line and
        hence greater sensitivity to the bull market
      • Funds that write options have greater sensitivity to the market when it
        is falling than when it is rising
      • Nonlinear characteristic lines suggest many hedge funds are implicit
        option writers

                                   Are hedge funds writing options?



                       FINS 3640                                      29
 Hedge Fund Performance and Changing
Factor Loadings: Option Trades Sh EFFIE
• Buying/selling options changes the distribution of returns, while static
  measures, like the SR, capture only certain components of the distribution
                        O
• Selling a covered call truncates the RHS of the distribution and results in
  negative skewness, which is undesirable. This is not captured by the SR.
• Factor regressions can also be manipulated. Furthermore they can be
  amplified by leverage
• Many common dynamic hedge fund strategies can be considered to be
  replicating option payoffs. Also
     • Monoline insurers selling protection on CDO products [no remaining AAA left except
       Berkshire Hathaway Insurance Corp, started in 2007]
     • LTCM, bailed out 1998
     • AIG selling protection via CDS, bailed out 2008

30                                       FINS 3640                                      30
 Performance: Non-Linear Factor Exposure
• Jurek and Stafford
  (2011) show that the
  performance of hedge                  Source: Figure 2, Jurek and Stafford (2011)

  funds can be matched,
  in aggregate, by a
  simple strategy of
  writing (naked) out-of-
  the-money puts, and
  taking leverage. The
  implied fee for this
  strategy is 3.6%, very
  close to the average HF
  fee of 3.4%.

32                                                             FINS 3640                                                    32
  Tail Events and Hedge Fund Performance
• Nassim Taleb:
   • Many hedge funds rack up fame through strategies
     that make money most of the time, but expose
     investors to rare but extreme losses (“The Black
     Swan”, “Fooled by randomness”)



• Examples:
   • The Black swan in Australia
   • The October 1987 crash: 20% drop in one day!
   • Long term capital management: Convergence trades
     on sovereign fixed income… but in 1998, Russia
     defaulted on its debt and prices diverged instead of
     converging. In august, total return loss was almost 10
     standard deviations of annual returns.


                                        FINS 3640             33
 Contracts: High Fees
     • Standard 2/20: expense ratio of 2%, performance fee of 20%. Renaissance
       Medallion is 5/44
     • Performance fee is calculated net of a benchmark, e.g. LIBOR (cash
       benchmark for absolute returns) or the S&P500
     • A high-water mark is often applied to the performance fee. Performance
       fees are levied only when the NAV exceeds the highest NAV previously
       achieved, eg NAV(0) = 100, NAV(1) = 120, NAV(2) = 110. No performance
       fee is payable at t=2 and none applies until NAV > 120.
     • Sometimes the incentive fee is paid only if performance is above a hurdle
       rate
     • Sometimes withdrawal fees are charged
     • Incentive fees are linked to higher performance: Ackermann et al. (1999)
       report a SR increase of 0.15 moving from a fund with no performance fee
       to a 20% performance fee with no increase in volatility

 Contracts: Limited Liquidity
• Minimum investments, e.g. $5 or $10 million
• Redemption is permitted typically only quarterly. Often HFs impose
  redemption notice periods, where an investor must wait 3 months, say,
  after having giving notice before the money is received.
• Lockup provisions, often 2 years, where investors cannot withdraw money
• Gates provisions: if there are “too many” redemption requests, the HF
  manager can limit redemptions. Often invoked when more than 20% of
  the fund is being redeemed, and redemptions are pro-rated.
• Suspensions: if a fund is dying, redemptions can be suspended in their
  entirety due to gates or suspensions

• Contrast 40-Act funds, which must provide (at least) daily liquidity

                                      FINS 3640                                  36
                  Fee Structure in Hedge Funds
• Incentive fees are effectively call options on the portfolio with:
      X(strike) = (Portfolio value) × (1 + Benchmark return)
                       so
                                    Citrfforreso
                                    M

• The manager gets the fee if the portfolio value rises sufficiently, but
  loses nothing if it falls

                                                  • incentive fee equal to 20% of
                                                    investment profits.
                                                  • Benchmark against risk-free



                                  FINS 3640

 Fee Structure in Hedge Funds

• High water mark
  • The fee structure can give incentives
    to shut down a poorly performing
    fund
     • If a fund experiences losses, it
       may not be able to charge an
       incentive unless it recovers to
       its previous higher value
     • With deep losses, this may be
       too difficult so the fund closes

  Fee Structure in Hedge Funds

• Funds of funds (feeder funds)
   • Hedge funds that invest in one or more other funds, providing an opportunity
     to diversify across hedge funds
   • Supposed to provide due diligence in screening funds for investment
     worthiness




                                    FINS 3640                                   39
 Fee Structure in Hedge Funds
  • Funds of funds (cont’d.)
     • Pay an incentive fee to each underlying fund that
       outperforms its benchmark even if the aggregate
       performance is poor. The investor can lose money and still
       pay incentive fees!
        • Diversification can hurt the investor

     • In theory, funds of funds spread risk across several
       different funds, but operate with considerable leverage (on
       top of the primary funds themselves)

     • If the various hedge funds in which these funds of funds
       invest have similar investment styles, diversification may be
       illusory                FINS 3640                               40
                         Example: Incentive fees in funds of funds
         • A fund of funds has $3 million invested in three hedge funds
         • Hurdle rate for the incentive fee is a zero return
         • Each fund charges an incentive fee of 20%
         • The (before fees) return of the aggregate portfolio of the fund of funds is -5%
         • Still investors pay incentive fees of $.12 for every $3 invested

 The economic role of hedge funds
• In efficiently inefficient markets what is the role of hedge funds?
   • Make capital markets more efficient
   • Market allocates resources in the economy so this is vital for the economy.
     Makes capital cheaper for good firms (with good uses for capital) and more
     expensive to bad ones (incentivizing a shift in their resources)
   • Improves CEO decisions (more informative prices and more monitoring by
     active investors)
   • Provide liquidity to markets
   • Diversifying returns for investors

 Malkiel and Saha (2005) main conclusions
• Hedge funds have bigger survivorship bias than mutual funds (about twice as much) plus a backfill
  bias that mutual funds do not have

• Hedge funds have higher attrition rates (75% die during the 8-year sample)

• No persistence in performance

• The risk of hedge funds is clearly non-normal

• A side note: In 2004 more than half of trades were made by hedge funds

• Still: correcting for the bias, hedge funds have low volatility and betas, so they provide interesting
  alphas for investors.

     Appendix A:
Exemption from 40-Act
 Definition
    • Hedge funds are private investment vehicles open to a
        limited number of wealthy or professional investors. They
        are characterized by
          • Limited number of investors. Investors must be accredited or
            qualified purchasers (legal terms for “rich, sophisticated investors”).
            Minimum capital requirements are high.
          • Usually have limited liquidity, ie limit access to invested capital
          • Often employ leverage and use derivatives. They invest in a wide
            range of strategies often not available in other investment vehicles.
          • Manager fees are very high and often have a performance
            component
          • Are [relatively] unregulated [Dodd-Frank 2010 will change this]
          • Are not transparent


51
 Appendix A: Exemption from 40-Act
• HFs usually take the form of a limited partnership or limited liability
  company or trust depending on the domicile
     • Master/feeder structure to minimize taxes for different parties, particularly
       US taxes of the earnings of foreign investors
     • In the US, hedge funds are structured as limited partnerships
        • General partner (GP): responsible for day-to-day management
        • Limited partner (LP): investors liable only for amount they invest
• HFs are structured so they are exempt from regulation under the
     • Investment Company Act of 1940: Comply with § 3(c)(1) or § 3(c)(7)
     • Securities Act 1933: firms issuing publicly traded securities overseen by the
       SEC
